blocks.content: On the far side of the funhouse, the group emerged into a quieter section of the fairgrounds — the game booths thinning out, more open space, the crowd redistributed elsewhere. The Ferris wheel was closer here. More visible.

The natural pull of individual interests began to work on the group's cohesion. Nathan wanted to find the horse racing booth he'd been talking about since last night. Maya wanted to circle back to the food stands. Lena announced, with the vagueness of something deliberate, that she needed to sit down for a minute.

None of this was rushed. None of it was obvious. The group simply began to become smaller versions of itself — the way five people eventually stop being a unit and become their smaller configurations. It happened the way it was always going to happen.
Cassandra and Ethan slowed their pace as the others drifted.
Lena glanced back as she moved toward the bench area near the quieter rides. She and Cassandra's eyes met for one second.
Lena nodded. Just barely. Just enough.
The group had rearranged itself. The afternoon had opened a space.
